ground_truth.title: Financially Savvy Home Renovations: Plan, Budget, and Build
ground_truth.author: Crystal Lee Butler, MBA
ground_truth.main_content: Your home is more than just a place to live—it's where life happens. With today's housing market still adjusting to challenges that resulted from COVID-19, high inflation, and rising interest rates, many homeowners are choosing to invest in the spaces they already have rather than searching for something new. On average, homeowners live in one home for about 12 years and own roughly three homes in their lifetime. If buying a new home isn't in the cards for you this year, a renovation or home improvement project could be a great way to enhance both the comfort and functionality of your space. Here are some important personal and financial considerations to keep in mind when budgeting for home improvement projects and creating your perfect oasis. Where you are in your homeownership journey, you might be feeling the urge to refresh or upgrade your space. Common reasons to renovate include being locked into a low mortgage rate, having built significant home equity, facing low housing inventory, wanting complete creative control, or having changed needs such as expanding your family or starting a home business. Home renovations can vary widely in cost—from a few hundred dollars for small updates to six-figure investments for major remodels. Consider your budget as it relates to your home's current value, potential value, and other financial priorities. Focus on what will add value to your home: renovations with the highest return on investment include kitchen remodels, deck additions, siding or roofing replacements, bathroom remodels, and primary suite additions. Projects that may decrease value include in-ground swimming pools, wall-to-wall carpeting, built-in electronics, and garage conversions. Once you have a project picked out, gather estimates from at least three different contractors. Search for contractors by asking for recommendations, reading reviews, verifying licensing and insurance, and asking about their experience. With an idea of how much your project will cost, figure out how to pay for it. If you've been saving for a home but decided to renovate instead, those funds might be a natural fit. If you don't have dedicated savings, explore financing options like a home equity line of credit, or HELOC, which leverages your home as collateral and typically offers favorable terms. Whether you choose to save ahead or use financing, consider how your renovation will fit into your overall financial picture. Taking on additional debt could impact your credit score or make it harder to save for long-term goals like retirement.
with: Home renovations can vary widely in cost—from a few hundred dollars for small updates to six-figure investments for major remodels.
Renovations with the highest value compared to cost include kitchen remodels, deck addition, replacing the siding or roofing, bathroom remodels or additions, and primary suite addition.
A home equity line of credit, or HELOC, can be used to access that equity as (just like it sounds) a line of credit.
Taking on additional debt, for example, could impact your credit score or make it harder to save for long-term goals like retirement.
Where you are in your homeownership journey, you might be feeling the urge to refresh or upgrade your space.
